title: Cohomologous Symplectic Forms with Different Gromov Widths
speaker: Shengzhen Ning
abstract: For closed symplectic manifolds, the Gromov width is among the most fundamental symplectic capacities: it measures the size of the largest embedded symplectic ball and is sensitive to the perturbation of the symplectic forms. A natural question, posed as Problem 46 in McDuff-Salamon's problem list, asks whether there are cohomologous symplectic forms with different Gromov widths. In this talk, I will explain how to obtain such examples in dimension 6 from dimension 4, by adapting Ruan's early example of deformation inequivalent symplectic forms.

proof_stages.label: Problem of width on a fixed cohomology class
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines Gromov width as a function on the space of symplectic forms, recalls local constancy on a fixed cohomology class, and formulates the McDuff-Salamon question asking whether cohomologous forms can have different widths.

proof_stages.label: Four-dimensional Kodaira dichotomy and width bounds
proof_stages.summary: Symplectic Kodaira dimension is reviewed as a smooth invariant of four-manifolds, with the crucial contrast that rational/ruled kappa=-infinity manifolds are uniruled and give Gromov-width upper bounds, while small exotic CP^2 blow-ups have kappa=2.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic cone comparison
proof_stages.summary: Using the Li-Liu symplectic cone theorem for b_2^+=1 and uniqueness up to sign of the canonical class in the kappa=2 case, the standard rational surface cone is compared as a wall-bounded subcone of the larger exotic cone under an identification of H^2.

proof_stages.label: Blow-up translation to a four-dimensional width gap
proof_stages.summary: Symplectic blow-up and blow-down identify ball-embedding size with the achievable area of the exceptional class, and the strict cone inclusion for one-point blow-ups yields homeomorphic but non-diffeomorphic four-manifolds with corresponding cohomology classes and different Gromov widths.

proof_stages.label: Stabilization to one smooth six-manifold
proof_stages.summary: After taking products with S^2, the classification of simply connected torsion-free six-manifolds realizes the chosen H^2-lattice isomorphism by a diffeomorphism, so the product symplectic forms can be pulled back to the same six-manifold as cohomologous forms.

proof_stages.label: Strict width inequality after stabilization
proof_stages.summary: Choosing the S^2 area large gives a lower bound from the exotic four-dimensional width, while a lifted uniruled class from the standard rational surface gives an upper bound, and the cohomology class is chosen so these bounds are strictly separated.

proof_stages.label: First-Chern and contact byproducts
proof_stages.summary: The same kappa=-infinity versus kappa=2 sign dichotomy yields cohomologous six-dimensional symplectic forms with different first Chern classes, which in integral monotone cases produces Boothby-Wang contact forms with the same Reeb vector field but different contact structures.

examples.name: Compactified cylinder for nonsqueezing
examples.note: Compactifying the cylinder to an S^2-factor times a torus illustrates how nonsqueezing can be read as an upper bound on Gromov width from a homology class.
examples.engagement_reason: he explicitly compactifies the cylinder’s Euclidean factor to a torus and reformulates nonsqueezing as a Gromov-width bound for the resulting closed manifold

examples.name: S^2 x S^2 width formula
examples.note: The formula that the width is the minimum of the two factor areas shows concretely that Gromov width varies when one does not fix the cohomology class.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the known width formula for S^2 x S^2 as the minimum of the two factor areas but does not derive it

passes.reason: The homogeneous product S^2 x S^2 is used to show the Gromov-width function is not constant even at fixed volume.

examples.name: Stabilized ordinary and exotic K3 surfaces
examples.note: Ordinary K3 surfaces with hyperkähler forms and exotic K3 copies give another stabilized source of cohomologous symplectic forms with different first Chern classes.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the stabilization construction for ordinary and exotic K3 surfaces, using the K3 cone/Torelli input to get cohomologous forms and comparing their first Chern classes

passes.reason: The construction uses an exotic smooth K3 and is used to refute the higher-dimensional analogue of cohomologous forms having the same first Chern class.
